Theory of real numbers, functions of one variable, sequences, limits, continuity and differentiation; continuity and differentiability of functions of several variables. Those who plan to do graduate work in mathematics should take MAA 4211. Credit will be given for, at most, one of MAA 4102, MAA 4211, or MAA 5104.
prerequisites: Prereq: (MAC 2313 or MAC 3474) and (MAS 4105 or MAS 3114) with minimum grades of C.

description: Continues the advanced calculus for engineers and physical scientists sequence. Theory of integration, transcendental functions and infinite series. MAA 4102 is not recommended for those who plan to do graduate work in mathematics; these students should take MAA 4212. Credit will be given for, at most, one of MAA 4103, MAA 4212 and MAA 5105.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAA 4102 with minimum grade of C.

description: Continues the advanced calculus sequence in limits, differentiation, integration and series. Credit will be given for, at most, one of MAA 4212, MAA 4103 and MAA 5105.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAA 4211 with minimum grade of C, taken the previous semester.

description: Continues the modern analysis sequence discussing the topology of metric spaces, numerical sequences and series, continuity, differentiation, the Riemann-Stieltjes integral, sequences and series of functions, the Stone-Weierstrass theorem, functions of several variables, Stokes' theorem and the Lebesgue theory. Credit will be given for, at most, MAA 4227 or MAA 5229.
prerequisites: Credits: 3; Prereq: MAA 4226 with minimum grade of C, taken the previous semester.

description: Complex numbers, analytic functions, Cauchy-Riemann equations, harmonic functions, elementary functions, integration, Cauchy-Goursat theorem, Cauchy integral formula, infinite series, residues and poles, conformal mapping. Credit will be given for, at most, MAA 4402 or MAA 5404.
prerequisites: Prereq: (MAC 2313 or MAC 3474) and MAP 2302 with minimum grades of C.
instructors: Paul Robinson, Luca Di Cerbo, Eric Evert, Frank Garvan

course_name: Advanced Calculus for Engineers and Physical Scientists I
description: Advanced Calculus for Engineers and Physical Scientists I

course_name: Advanced Calculus for Engineers and Physical Scientists II
description: Advanced Calculus for Engineers and Physical Scientists II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAA 5104.

description: Modern Analysis II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAA 5228.

course_name: Introduction to Complex Variables for Engineers and Physical Scientists
description: Introduction to Complex Variables for Engineers and Physical Scientists
instructors: Luca Di Cerbo, Paul Robinson, Frank Garvan, Eric Evert

description: Continuation of MAA 6616 Analysis I.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAA 6616.

description: In this course, students will develop problem solving skills, critical thinking, computational proficiency, and contextual fluency through the study of equations, functions, and their graphs. Emphasis will be placed on quadratic, exponential, and logarithmic functions. Topics will include solving equations and inequalities, definition and properties of a function, domain and range, transformations of graphs, operations on functions, composite and inverse functions, basic polynomial and rational functions, exponential and logarithmic functions, and applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: completion of the ALEKS placement exam.
instructors: Lezhi Liu, Leo Adaryukov, Kirby Brown

description: Trigonometric functions and their properties and graphs, inverse trigonometric functions and their properties and graphs, trigonometric identities, conditional trigonometric equations, solutions of triangles, and applications.
instructors: George Roman, Zachariah Ross, Fatima Akinola, Justin Young, Brendan Williams, David Maynoldi

description: College algebra, functions, coordinate geometry, exponential and logarithmic functions. (M)
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 1105 with a minimum grade of C or (ALEKS >=46%).

description: College algebra, functions, coordinates geometry, exponential and logarithmic functions, and trigonometry. This fast-paced course is designed as a review of algebra and trigonometry to prepare the student for calculus. This course assumes prior knowledge of intermediate algebra (algebra 2) and trigonometry.
prerequisites: Prereq: ALEKS >=61%, if taken January 1, 2020 or later, or ALEKS >= 50, if taken before January 1, 2020.
instructors: Patrick Carmichael, Okon Emmanuel, Satyanath Howladar

description: Geometric and heuristic approach to calculus; differentiation and integration of simple algebraic and exponential functions; applications to graphing, marginal analysis, optimization, areas and volumes.
prerequisites: Prereq: Any of: min acceptable score on the online math placement exam; min grade of C in MAC1140 or higher; AP cr. for MAC2311; IB cr. for MAC1140 or higher. Course grades, AP/IB scores used to meet this prereq must be on file at UF by registration.
instructors: ALEXANDER YORK, Libin Rong, Valentino Vito, Larissa Williamson

description: Sequences, geometric and Taylor series; systems of linear equations, Gaussian elimination, matrices, determinants and vectors; partial differentiation, multiple integrals; applications to marginal analysis, least-squares and Lagrange multipliers. (M)
prerequisites: Credits: 3; Prereq: MAC 2233 with minimum grade of C, or the equivalent.

description: In this course, students will develop problem solving skills, critical thinking, computational proficiency, and contextual fluency through the study of limits, derivatives, and definite and indefinite integrals of functions of one variable, including algebraic, exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions, and applications. Topics will include limits, continuity, differentiation and rates of change, optimization, curve sketching, and introduction to integration and area.
prerequisites: Prereq: Any of: min. acceptable score on the online math placement exam; a grade of C in MAC1147 or higher; AP cr. for MAC 2311; IB cr. for MAC1147 or higher. Course grades, AP/IB scores used to meet this prereq must be on file at UF by registration.
instructors: John Streese, Maguette Beye, Julian Michele, Aleksandr Mkrtchyan, Cayley Robinson, Laureline Polli, Quinn Hartman, Jensen Bridges, Jenna Fant, Aritram Dhar

description: Techniques of integration; applications of integration; differentiation and integration of inverse trigonometric, exponential and logarithmic functions; sequences and series. (Note: Credit will be given for at most one of MAC 2312, MAC 2512 and MAC 3473.)
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2311 or MAC 3472 with a minimum grade of C.
instructors: Kwai-Lee Chui, Stephen Adams, Carl Ye, Parisa Azizi, Avi Mukhopadhyay, Joseph Litvin, Saketh Narayanan, Jack Chou, Lauren Craddock, Minseo Cho, Harris Barton, Robin Maier, Harshika Rathi, Madeline Berryman-Dages, Emma Thomas, Theodosios Aivalis, Vangmay Jayant, Nolan Ison, Joshua Arroyo

description: Solid analytic geometry, vectors, partial derivatives, multiple integrals. (Note: Credit will be given for at most one of MAC 2313 and MAC 3474.)
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2312 or MAC 2512 or MAC 3473 with a minimum grade of C.
instructors: Missy Shabazz, Alexander Wong, Erik Shute, Willard Keeran, Thomas Garrison, Collin Stebbins, Luke Fontan, Abby Owens White, Michael Waite, Xiaochen Duan, Vincent Holmlund, Umesha Wijerathne, Hemaho Beaugard Taboe, Michael Maiello, Maitreyo Bhattacharjee, Jasen Lai, Aditya De Saha

description: Introduces mathematical computation and the Python programming language. Emphasizes using mathematical algorithms to solve problems in analysis, number theory, combinatorics, algebra, linear algebra, numerical analysis, and probability.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2311 or MAC 3472, minimum grade of C.

description: Matching theory, block designs, finite projective planes and error-correcting codes. Does not require MAD 4203.
prerequisites: Prereq: (MAC 2312 or MAC 2512 or MAC 3473) and (MAS 3300 or MHF 3202) with minimum grades of C.

description: Theory and applications of graphs including connectivity, planarity, coloration, algorithms, and network flow.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAS 3300 with a minimum grade of C or MHF 3202 with a minimum grade of C.

description: Numerical integration, nonlinear equations, linear and nonlinear systems of equations, differential equations and interpolation.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAS 3114 or MAS 4105 with a minimum grade of C and experience with a scientific programming language.
instructors: Jason Harrington, Nan Jiang, Dylan Connell

description: Numerical techniques to solve systems of nonlinear equations to approximate functions, to compute derivatives, to evaluate integrals, and to integrate systems of differential equations. Introduction to numerical techniques for partial differential equations. Companion to MAD 6406.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAA 4212, MAA 5105, or MAA 5229; and programming language.

description: Topics in Combinatorial Theory II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAD 7396.

description: Supervised Teaching
prerequisites: Prereq: consent of graduate adviser.

description: Internship in College Teaching
prerequisites: Prereq: consent of graduate adviser.

description: First-order ordinary differential equations, theory of linear ordinary differential equations, solution of linear ordinary differential equations with constant coefficients, the Laplace transform and its application to solving linear ordinary differential equations. (M)
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2312 or MAC 2512 or MAC 3473 with a minimum grade of C.
instructors: ALEXANDER YORK, Carol Demas, Chamila Malagoda Gamage, Wanchen Zhao, David Groisser, Larissa Williamson

description: Random walks and Poisson processes, martingales, Markov chains, Brownian motion, stochastic integrals and Ito's formula.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4321 with a minimum grade of C.

course_name: Differential Equations for Engineers and Physical Scientists
description: The second course in differential equations. Topics include systems of linear differential equations, stability theory and phase plane analysis, power series solutions of differential equations, Sturm-Liouville boundary-value problems and special functions. Credit will be given for, at most, MAP 4305 or MAP 5304.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAP 2302 and (MAS 3114 or MAS 4105 or EGM 3344) with minimum grades of C.
instructors: Thialita Nascimento, Eros M Sunny, Maia Martcheva, Paul Robinson, Jacob Levenson

description: General theory of initial value problems, linear systems and phase portraits, linearization of nonlinear systems, stability and bifurcation theory, an introduction to chaotic dynamics
prerequisites: Prereq: MAP 2302 and (MAS 3114 or MAS 4105) with minimum grades of C.

description: Introduces second-order linear partial differential equations (heat, wave and Laplace equations), separation of variables in PDEs, Sturm-Liouville eigenvalue problems, method of eigenfunction expansions (Fourier analysis) and Green's functions. Possible introduction to first-order PDEs and the method of characteristics. Credit will be given for, at most, MAP 4341 or MAP 5345.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAP 2302 and MAP 4305 with minimum grades of C.

description: Introduces linear systems and transforms; Laplace, Fourier and Z transforms and their mutual relationship; convolutions. Operational calculus; computational methods including the fast Fourier transform; second-order stationary processes and their autocorrelation functions; and problems of interpolation, extrapolation, filtering and smoothing of second-order stationary processes.
prerequisites: Prereq: (MAC 2313 or MAC 3474) and MAP 2302 and (MAS 3114 or MAS 4105) with minimum grades of C.

course_name: Intermediate Differential Equations for Engineers and Physical Scientists
description: Intermediate Differential Equations for Engineers and Physical Scientists
Introduction to Partial Differential Equations
Unconstrained and constrained optimization, linear and nonlinear programming, gradient, multiplier, and quasi-Newton methods. Penalty, multiplier, and projection methods for constrained problems.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAD 6406 and MAD 6407 or consent of instructor.

description: Theory and methods for solving linear and nonlinear systems of differential equations and partial differential equations. Applications and computer techniques included.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAA 5229.

description: Partial Differential Equations II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAP 6356.

description: Probability and Potential Theory II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAP 6472

description: Continuation of MAP 6487.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAP 6487.

course_name: Mathematical Methods of Physics and Engineering II
description: Mathematical Methods of Physics and Engineering II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAP 6505.

description: Mathematical research on projects sponsored by a university laboratory or an off-campus industrial internship program.
prerequisites: Prereq: consent of supervisory committee chair.

description: Linear equations, matrices and determinants. Vector spaces and linear transformations. Inner products and eigenvalues. Emphasizes computational aspects of linear algebra.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2312, MAC 2512 or MAC 3473 with a minimum grade of C and experience with a scientific programming language.
instructors: Shu Huang, Kushagri Sharma, Dmitrii Smorchkov, Iris Shi, Amit Bhat, Kyle Adams, Michelle Baker

description: Linear equations, matrices, vector spaces, linear transformations, determinants, eigenvalues and inner-product spaces. Includes both theory and computational skills. Develops the ability to reason through, and coherently write, proofs of theorems. For math majors, this course serves as a transition from a study of techniques into more conceptual math; for engineering and science majors, it serves also as a coherent foundation in linear algebra.
prerequisites: Prereq: (MAC 2313 or MAC 3474) and (MAS 3300 or MHF 3202) with minimum grades of C.
instructors: Chunmei Wang, Xingjian Xu, Willard Keeran, Jacob Kowalczyk, Henry Adams, Darren Schmidt, Peter Sin, Nan Jiang

description: A second course in linear algebra, focusing on topics that are the most essential for data science. Introduces theory and numerical methods required for large data-sets and machine learning. Topics include LU, QR, and singular-value decompositions; conditioning and stability; the DFT and filters; deep learning; fully connected and convolutional nets.
prerequisites: Prereq: (MAS 3114 or MAS 4105) and MAC 2313.

description: Introduces elementary number theory and its applications to computer science and cryptology. Divisibility, primes, Euclidean Algorithm, congruences, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Euler-Fermat Theorem and primitive roots. Selected applications to decimal fractions, continued fractions, computer file storage and hashing functions, and public-key cryptography.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2312 and (MAC 2512 or MAC 3473) with a minimum grade of C; MAS 3300 recommended.

description: Sets and mappings, groups and subgroups, homomorphisms and isomorphisms, permutations, rings and domains, arithmetic properties of domains, and fields. Requires facility in writing proofs.
prerequisites: Prereq: (MAS 3300 or MHF 3202 with a minimum grade of B) or MAS 4105 with a minimum grade of C.
instructors: Alexander Berkovich, Zachary Hamaker, Alexandre Turull

description: A second course in Abstract Algebra, focusing on Galois Theory, the algebraic theory of fields and polynomial equations. Introduces concepts of abstract algebra used in settling famous historical problems including the problems of angle trisection and duplication of cubes by ruler and compass constructions, and the insolubility of polynomial equations of the fifth degree by radicals.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAS 4301.

description: Algebra II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAS 6331.

description: Theory of Numbers II
prerequisites: Prereq: MAS 7215.

description: Topics in Algebra II
Special topics not obtainable in regular course offerings.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2313 or MAC 3474 with a minimum grade of C and undergraduate coordinator permission.

description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in mathematics. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in mathematics.
instructors: Henry Adams, Jason Harrington, Youngmin Park, Sara Pollock, Chunmei Wang, Sergei Shabanov, Arnaud Marsiglietti, Tracy Stepien, Jeremy Booher

course_name: Special Topics in Mathematics: INTRO ALGEBRA 2
description: Qualified undergraduates take part in seminars or classes on special topics.
prerequisites: Prereq: undergraduate coordinator permission.

description: Individual Work
Supervised Research
instructors: Miklos Bona, Luca Di Cerbo, DANA BARTOSOVA, Youngmin Park

course_name: Special Topics in Mathematics: SEM IN TOPOLOGY
description: Special Topics in Mathematics
prerequisites: Prereq: consent of graduate adviser, who should be consulted well in advance of registration.

description: Research for Master's Thesis

Research for doctoral students before admission to candidacy. Designed for students with a master's degree in the field of study or for students who have been accepted for a doctoral program. Not appropriate for students who have been admitted to candidacy.
instructors: Hubert Wagner, Cheng Yu, Alexandre Turull, Robin Tucker-Drob, Helen Moore, Michael Jury, Alexander Dranishnikov, Miklos Bona, Youngmin Park, Henry Adams, Kevin Knudson, Eric Evert, Chunmei Wang, Andrew Vince

description: Research for Doctoral Dissertation
instructors: Sara Pollock, Jeremy Booher, Peter Bubenik, Alexander Berkovich, Henry Adams, Krishnaswami Alladi, Michael Jury, Jindrich Zapletal, Kevin Keating, Calistus Ngonghala, Luca Di Cerbo, Alexander Dranishnikov, Yeor Hafuta, Scott Mccullough, Sergei Pilyugin, William Hager, Zachary Hamaker, Lei Zhang, Frank Garvan, Hubert Wagner, Miklos Bona

description: In this course, students will utilize multiple means of problem solving through student-centered mathematical exploration. The course is designed to teach students to think more effectively and increase their problem-solving ability through practical application and divergent thinking. This course is appropriate for students in a wide range of disciplines/programs.

Fundamentals of logic, set theory, and proof techniques. Topics include propositional and predicate logic, set operations, functions, relations, mathematical induction, and proof strategies. Emphasis on analyzing, constructing, and writing rigorous mathematical arguments with clarity and precision.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2312 with a minimum grade of C.
instructors: Jesse Kim, Luca Di Cerbo, Ross Ptacek, Andrew Vince, Libin Rong, Vincent Vatter

description: Basic axioms and concepts of set theory. Students present proofs. Credit will be given for, at most, MHF 4102 or MHF 5107.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAS 4105 with a minimum grade of C.

description: Basic axioms and concepts of set theory, axiom of choice, Zorn's lemma, Schroder-Bernstein theorem, cardinal numbers, ordinal numbers, and the continuum hypothesis.

The three sub-areas Model theory, computability theory, and set theory will be covered. Specific topics may include axiomatization of set theory, transfinite recursion and cardinal arithmetic, Goedel's constructible universe, Cohen's method of forcing, Turing degrees, and Post's problem.
prerequisites: Prereq: MHF 6306.

description: Axiomatic treatment of topics in Euclidean, non-Euclidean, projective geometry and (time permitting) fractal geometry. Particularly useful for prospective secondary-school mathematics teachers.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2312 and (MAC 2512 or MAC 3473 with a minimum grade of C).

description: Continues the basic concepts of general topology. Credit will be given for, at most, MTG 4303 or MTG 5317.
prerequisites: Prereq: MTG 4302 with a minimum grade of C.

description: Introduction to Topology II
prerequisites: Prereq: MTG 5316.

description: Differential Geometry II
prerequisites: Prereq: MTG 6256.

description: Topology II
prerequisites: Prereq: MTG 6346.

description: Discussion of advanced topics in topology and its applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: MTG 7396.

description: In this course, students will utilize descriptive and inferential statistical methods in contextual situations, using technology as appropriate. The course is designed to increase problem-solving abilities and data interpretation through practical applications of statistical concepts. This course is appropriate for students in a wide range of disciplines and programs.

course_name: The Data Analytics Revolution In Sports
description: Explores the data-driven decision process within the field of athletics and game play using data analytics and visualization techniques. Compares the processes used in sports with those used in business and everyday life and illustrates examples of how data can be used to understand human behavior.

An introduction to the analysis of variance. Nonparametric statistical methods and applications. Analysis of count data: chi-square and contingency tables. Simple and multiple linear regression methods with applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 2023 or the equivalent.

description: Basic concepts in probability, statistics, engineering applications. Use of computational methods, logic, and reasoning. Formulation of mathematical models, application of statistical techniques, and solution of real-world problems. Topics include probability, random variables, estimation, hypothesis testing, correlation, regression, and analysis of variance.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2311.

description: Introduction to statistical computing and programming with data. Topics include basic programming in R; data types and data structures in R; importing and cleaning data; specifying statistical models in R; statistical graphics; statistical simulation using pseudo-random numbers; reproducible research and the documentation of statistical analyses.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 2023 with a minimum grade of B or (STA 3024 or STA 3032 with a minimum grade of B-) or (AP statistics score of 4 or 5).

description: Overview of modern statistical modeling. Topics include linear regression, binary regression and classification, cross-validation, nonlinear regression and smoothing, tree-based methods, the bootstrap, and causal inference. Approaches will be illustrated in R.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 3100.

description: Simple linear regression and multiple linear regression models. Inference about model parameters and predictions, diagnostic, and remedial measures about the model, independent variable selection, multicollinearity, autocorrelation, and nonlinear regression.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 3100 and (STA 3024 or STA 3032 or STA 4321 or MAS 3114 or MAS 4105).

description: The basic principles of experimental design: analysis of variance for experiments with a single factor; randomized blocks and Latin square designs: multiple comparison of treatment means; factorial and nested designs; analysis of covariance; response surface methodology.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4210.

description: Overview of the field of statistical learning. Topics include linear regression, classification, resampling methods, shrinkage approaches, tree-based methods, support vector machines, and clustering. Approaches will be illustrated in R.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4322 and STA 4210 and (MAS 3114 or 4105).

description: Overview of computational statistics and how to implement the methods in R. Topics include Monte Carlo methods in inference, bootstrap, permutation tests, and Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4210 and STA 4322 and (MAS 3114 or MAS 4105).

description: Introduces the theory of probability, counting rules, conditional probability, independence, additive and multiplicative laws, Bayes Rule. Discrete and continuous random variables, their distributions, moments and moment generating functions. Multivariate probability distributions, independence, covariance. Distributions of functions of random variables, sampling distributions, central limit theorem.
prerequisites: Prereq: MAC 2313 or MAC 3474 with a minimum grade of C.
instructors: Yeison Quiceno Duran, James Hobert, John Streese

description: Sampling distributions, central limit theorem, estimation, properties of point estimators, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, common large sample tests, normal theory small sample tests, uniformly most powerful and likelihood ratio tests, linear models and least squares, correlation. Introduction to analysis of variance.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4321 or the equivalent.

description: Description and inference using proportions and odds ratios, multi-way contingency tables, logistic regression and other generalized linear models, log-linear models applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 3100 and (STA 3024 or STA 3032 or STA 4210 or STA 4322).

description: Review of matrix theory, univariate normal, t, chi-squared and F distributions and multivariate normal distribution. Inference about multivariate means including Hotelling's T2, multivariate analysis of variance, multivariate regression and multivariate repeated measures. Inference about covariance structure including principal components, factor analysis and canonical correlation. Multivariate classification techniques including discriminant and cluster analyses. Additional topics at the discretion of the instructor, time permitting.
prerequisites: Prereq: (STA 3024 or STA 4210 or STA 4322 or STA 6127 or STA 6167) and (MAS 3114 or MAS 4105 or the equivalent).

course_name: Introduction to Time Series and Forecasting
description: Stationarity, autocorrelation, ARMA models; frequency domain methods and the spectral density; forecasting methods; and computationally-oriented application to case studies.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4210 and STA 4321.

description: Special topics designed to meet the needs and interests of individual students.
prerequisites: Prereq: department permission.

course_name: Undergraduate Research in Statistics: RESEARCH STATISTICS
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application.
Supervised activity associated with planning and/or analyzing data from a research project. Supervision by a faculty member or delegated authority and a post-internship written report are required.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4211 and undergraduate coordinator permission.

course_name: Capstone in Statistics and Data Science
description: A comprehensive team-based learning experience where students synthesize what they have learned in previous core courses and apply that knowledge to new, complex situations working in partnership with professional data scientists and statisticians to analyze a real-world problem. Skills such as collaboration, data ethics and communication are heavily emphasized.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4210 and 3 additional credits in STA 4XXX and (BS or BA STA or DAT major or STA minor or ACS minor)

description: Topics in probability and statistics, particularly discrete and continuous random variables, sampling distributions, estimation, and hypothesis testing. Applications to engineering and natural science.
prerequisites: Prereq: grade of C or better in MAC 2313 and STA 3032 or equivalent.

description: Direct continuation of STA 4321/STA 5325. Basic material for distribution theory, sampling distributions, properties of estimators, hypothesis testing, linear regression analysis, and analysis of variance. A good knowledge of calculus is helpful.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 4321 or equivalent.

description: Description and inference using proportions and odds ratios, multi-way contingency tables, logistic regression and other generalized linear models, and loglinear models applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 3024, 3032, 4210, 4322, STA 6127, or STA 6167. Intended for graduate students not majoring in statistics.

description: Review of matrix theory, univariate normal, t, chi-squared and F distributions, and multivariate normal distributions. Inference about multivariate means, Hotelling's T2  multivariate analysis of variance, multivariate regression, and multivariate repeated measures. Inference about covariance structure, principal components, factor analysis, and canonical correlation. Multivariate classification techniques, discriminant and cluster analysis. Additional topics at the discretion of the instructor, time permitting.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 3024, STA 6127, STA 6167, or 4211. Intended for graduate students not majoring in statistics.

description: Stationarity, autocorrelation, ARMA models, non-stationary processes, ARIMA models, regression with ARMA errors, model-based forecasting, forecasting algorithms.
prerequisites: Coreq: STA 4322 or STA 5328.

description: Analysis of covariance and general linear model. Factorial, nested, split-plot, and incomplete block designs. Analysis of count data.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 6166.

description: Theory of Linear Models
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 6208, STA 6327, STA 6329.

course_name: Statistical Computing II: Monte Carlo Methods
description: Second of a two course sequence on statistical computing. Covers Monte Carlo (MC) methods, Monte Carlo methods based on Markov chains (MCMC), and variational inference. Describes implementations of these methods and classes of problems in Bayesian inference they can address. Also, discussion of convergence issues. R is the primary programming language.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 6326; Coreq: STA 6327.

description: Estimation and hypothesis testing. Sufficiency, information, estimation, maximum likelihood, confidence intervals, uniformly most powerful tests, likelihood ratio tests, sequential testing, univariate normal inference, decision theory, analysis of categorical data.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 6326.

description: Special topics designed to meet the needs and interests of individual students.
prerequisites: Prereq: departmental approval.

description: Supervised Research
Special topics of an advanced nature suitable for seminar treatment but not given in regular courses.
prerequisites: Prereq: departmental approval.

description: Introduces a variety of advanced methods used in modern regression analyses, including semiparametric regression, regularization and shrinkage estimators, and advanced statistical machine learning methods.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 7249.

description: Summability of independent random variables. Laws of large numbers. Convergence in distribution. Characteristic functions. Uniqueness and continuity theorems. The Lindeberg-Feller central limit theorem. Degenerate convergence criterion.
prerequisites: Prereq: STA 7466.

course_name: Special Topics in Statistics: High Dimensional Time-Series
description: Possible Topics: Smoothing Methods, Analysis of Longitudinal Data, Data Mining and Statistical Learning, Mixed Models, Theory and Methods, Resampling Methods, Functional Data Analysis.
prerequisites: Prereq: Permission of Graduate Coordinator.

description: Research for doctoral students before admission to candidacy. Designed for students with a master's degree in the field of study or for students who have been accepted for a doctoral program. Not appropriate for students who have been admitted to candidacy.
Research for Doctoral Dissertation

Emphasizes the practical applications of basic physics to a wide range of professions including architecture, agricultural sciences, building construction, and forest resources. Mechanics of motion, forces, energy, momentum, wave motion, and heat. (P) This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: algebra and trigonometry.

description: Laboratory experience illustrating the practical applications of basic physics, including the mechanics of motion, forces, energy, momentum, wave motion and heat. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Coreq: PHY 2004.
instructors: Charles Parks, Nivarthana Yapa Mudiyanselage, Charles Taylor, Austin Lam

description: Continuation of the sequence. Electric and magnetic fields. Geometrical, wave and applied optics. Modern and nuclear physics. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 2004 or equivalent.

description: Laboratory experience illustrating the practical applications of electric and magnetic fields geometrical, wave and applied optics; and modern and nuclear physics. (P) corequisite: PHY 2005.This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Coreq: PHY 2005.
instructors: Charles Parks, Nivarthana Yapa Mudiyanselage, Raju Sharma Khatiwada, Monique Kubovsky

description: This course offers a comprehensive survey of physics, covering a wide range of topics including motion, Newton's laws, energy, sound, heat, electricity, magnetism, and optics. Emphasizing a conceptual understanding of physics, the course integrates critical thinking skills and real-world applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: high school algebra and trigonometry or the equivalent.

description: Addresses the question of how the world's energy needs will be met based on available resources, technology, environmental concerns, economics, personal choices, and nation and international policy. Develops quantitative reasoning skills necessary to make informed decisions. Compares energy use, resources, and policy in different countries. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: Any Quest 1 course with a minimum grade of C.

description: This calculus-based course serves as the first in a two-part series, covering topics like kinematics, dynamics, energy, momentum, rotational motion, fluid dynamics, oscillatory motion, and waves. Designed for science and engineering majors, the course integrates critical thinking, analytical skills, and real-world applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: high-school physics, PHY 2020 or the equivalent, and MAC 2311. Coreq: MAC 2312.
instructors: Shawn Weatherford, Saroj Pokharel, Alexander Donald, Ethan Angerhofer, Kyle Tregoning, Ethan Huecker, Henry Hill, Thomas Caligiure, Conor Naughton, Emrah Simsek, Dylan Tagrin, Andrey Korytov, John Yelton, Diana Pedraza Gil, Carlos Ramos P.

description: Lab for Physics with Calculus 1. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: Degree-seeking students only. Coreq: PHY 2048 or the equivalent.
instructors: Charles Parks, Nivarthana Yapa Mudiyanselage, Caden Zaccardi, Wanyue Wang, Nicholas Maldonado, Andrew Valentini, Soobeom Chung, Bennett Hughes, Charles Taylor, Santiago Moreno Gonzalez, Chuks Onyeka, Luke Johnson, Jake Meuse, Aatif Kaisar Khan, Inkyu Hwang, Andrew Kinnion, Dhashin Krishna Murali

description: The second of a two-semester sequence of Physics for scientists and engineers. Content includes Coulombs law, electric fields and potentials, capacitance, currents and circuits, Amperes law, Faradays law, inductance, Maxwells equations, electromagnetic waves, ray optics, interference and diffraction. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 2048 and MAC 2312; Coreq: MAC 2313.
instructors: Ravin Kodikara, Selman Hershfield, Joshua Covey, LingQin Xue, David Sadek, Cristofer Caballeros, Matthew Reinhard, Yohei Ema

description: Laboratory experience for PHY 2049 illustrating the practical applications of Coulomb’s law, electric fields and potentials, capacitance, currents and circuits, Ampere’s law, Faraday’s law, inductance, Maxwell’s equations, electromagnetic waves, ray optics, interference and diffraction. (P) This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: Degree-seeking students only. Coreq: PHY 2049 or the equivalent.
instructors: Charles Parks, Caden Zaccardi, Nivarthana Yapa Mudiyanselage, Mateo Halka, Mengke Zhang, Nathaniel Bevins, Nhat Huy Tran, Qiushi Wei, Rachel Prempeh, Claire Chen, Ayan Dey, Raju Sharma Khatiwada, James Grossmann, Maximilian Smuda, John Paul Holt, Yuxin Zhao

description: This course is the first in a two-part series intended for non-physics majors, offering an algebra and trigonometry approach to topics such as kinematics, dynamics, energy, momentum, rotational motion, fluid dynamics, oscillatory motion, and waves. The course fosters analytical and critical thinking skills to promote a scientific understanding of the real world.
prerequisites: Prereq: high school algebra and trigonometry, or the equivalent. Degree-seeking students only.
instructors: Tarek Saab, BingKan Xue, Anguie Xiomara Gonzalez Duque, Alvaro Herrera, Jiabin Yu, Joseph Jung, Boran Yesilyurt, Antonios Kyriazis, Yu Zhang, Panagiotis Kotoulas

description: Laboratory experience for PHY 2053 illustrating the practical applications of the structure and properties of matter; kinematics, dynamics and statics; momentum and energy; rotation, elasticity; vibration; fluids; temperature and expansion, heat transfer, thermal behavior of gases; wave motion and sound. (P) This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Coreq: PHY 2053 or the equivalent. Degree-seeking students only.
instructors: Charles Parks, Caden Zaccardi, Nivarthana Yapa Mudiyanselage, Dhashin Krishna Murali, Samuel Sebastian, Sourin Dey, Andrew Kinnion, Austin Lam, Marwa Selim, Yuan Gao, Jake Meuse, Stephanie Montoya, Zachary Turner

description: Second semester of introductory physics de-emphasizing calculus. Electric charge, field, and circuits; electromagnetism, applied electricity; geometrical optics, wave optics, applied optics; electrons and photons; atoms and nuclei. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 2053 or the equivalent. Degree-seeking students only.
instructors: Sujata Krishna, James Hamlin, PAUL FULDA

description: Laboratory experience for PHY 2054 illustrating the practical applications of electric charge, fields and circuits; electromagnetism, applied electricity; geometrical optics, wave optics, applied optics; electrons and photons; atoms and nuclei. (P) This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Coreq: PHY 2054 or the equivalent. Degree-seeking students only.
instructors: Charles Parks, Caden Zaccardi, Nivarthana Yapa Mudiyanselage, James Grossmann, Monique Kubovsky, Juan Perez Mosquera, Qiushi Wei, Yuxin Zhao, Cedric Broussard, Odysseas Hilides, Jayani Wilegoda Mudalige, Ashwini Malviya, Xuce Niu, Luka Vukomanovic, Rachel Prempeh, Michael Taylor, Caleb Faison, Ayan Dey, John Paul Holt, Evan McCarthy, Mengke Zhang

description: First of the enriched sequence for physics majors and others wishing a deeper understanding of mechanics, kinematics, conservation laws, harmonic motion, central forces and special relativity. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: Degree-seeking students only; Coreq: MAC 2312 or the equivalent.

description: Second course of the enriched sequence. Electricity and magnetism, including electrostatics, Gauss' law, potentials, vector analysis, Laplace's equation, conductors and insulators, circuits, magnetism, Maxwell's equations and E&M fields in matter. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 2060 or instructor permission; Coreq: MAC 2313 or the equivalent.

description: Theory of relativity and introduction to quantum theory. Course includes wave mechanics, quantum theory of solids, nuclear and particle physics and cosmology.
prerequisites: Credits: 3; Prereq: PHY 2061 or instructor permission, and MAP 2302 or the equivalent.

description: Modern and atomic physics, relativity, wave phenomena and the basis of quantum physics.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 2049 or the equivalent.

description: First part of PHY 3221/4222 sequence in classical mechanics emphasizing matrices, vector calculus, Newtonian mechanics, frames of reference, conservation laws and harmonic oscillation.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 2049 or the equivalent; Coreq: MAP 2302 or the equivalent.

description: First part of the PHY 3323/4324 sequence in electromagnetism. Course covers static electric and magnetic fields, electric circuits, Maxwell's equations, radiation and propagation of electromagnetic waves.
prerequisites: Prereq: (PHY 2049 or PHY 2061, or the equivalent) or (MAP 2302 or the equivalent).

description: First part of the PHY 3513/4523 sequence that includes treatment of classical thermodynamics, including fundamental postulates, entropy and equations of states; thermodynamic equilibrium and potentials; Maxwell relations and phase transitions.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 2049 or PHY 2061.

description: Second part of the sequence in classical mechanics studying rigid body mechanics; motion in a non-inertial frame, Lagrangian and Hamiltonian dynamics; elements of fluid mechanics; and relativity theory.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 3221 and differential equations.

description: The second in the PHY 3323/4324 electromagnetism sequence studying static electric and magnetic fields, electric circuits, Maxwell's equations, radiation and propagation of electromagnetic waves.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 3323 and differential equations.

description: Second of the PHY 3513/4523 sequence. Introduction to statistical physics and continued study of classical thermodynamics, including fundamental postulates, entropy and equations of states; thermodynamic equilibrium and potentials; Maxwell relations and phase transitions.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 3513 and PHY 4604; differential equations.

description: First of the PHY 4604/4605 sequence. Basic concepts of quantum mechanics with applications in atomic and nuclear physics and condensed matter.
prerequisites: Prereq: (PHY 3101 or PHY 3063) and MAP 2302 or equivalent.

description: Second of the PHY 4604/4605 quantum mechanics sequence with applications in atomic and nuclear physics and condensed matter.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 4604.

description: Electronics in the laboratory.
prerequisites: Coreq: PHY 3323 or the equivalent.

description: Current laboratory techniques.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 4604 and PHY 4802L.
instructors: Heather Ray, Neil Sullivan, Xiao-Xiao Zhang

description: Qualified undergraduate students study selected topics in physics.
prerequisites: Prereq: 12 credits of physics and instructor permission.

course_name: Undergraduate Research in Physics: UG RES PHYSICS
description: Course provides firsthand, supervised research in Physics. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in Physics.
instructors: Siyao Xu, Amlan Biswas, Chunjing Jia, Jiabin Yu, DOMINIQUE LAROCHE, Edwin Eduardo Pedrozo Penafiel, BingKan Xue, James Hamlin, Yuta Takahashi, Gabriel Birzu, Yasumasa Takano, Philip Chang, Jeffrey Andrews

description: Assigned reading and problems program, special topics, or development of special experimental or theoretical problems. Work selected according to student's needs and interests.
prerequisites: Prereq: consent of instructor.

description: Continuation of PHY 6346.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 6346.

description: Equilibrium ensembles for classical and quantum systems, fluctuations, applications to normal fluids, phase transitions and critical phenomena, plasmas.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 6645 and PHY 6246.

description: Time dependent perturbation theory, scattering theory, identical particles and second quantization, Dirac equation.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 6645.

description: Treatment of an experimental or theoretical problem or topic assigned on the basis of student's needs and interests.
instructors: Yoonseok Lee, James Hamlin, Edwin Eduardo Pedrozo Penafiel, Jiabin Yu

description: Supervised Research
Summary presentation of contemporary topics by visiting and local researchers.
Path integral quantization; perturbation theory; renormalization; quantization of gauge fields; applications.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 6648.

description: Research for doctoral students before admission to candidacy. Designed for students with a master's degree in the field of study or for students who have been accepted for a doctoral program. Not appropriate for students who have been admitted to candidacy.
instructors: Philip Feng, Jiabin Yu, Yuta Takahashi, Gabriel Birzu, Tarek Saab, BingKan Xue, Siyao Xu, Philip Chang, Laura Blecha, Jacobo Konigsberg, Sergey Klimenko, Richard Hennig, Chunjing Jia, Ivan Furic, Edwin Eduardo Pedrozo Penafiel, Xiao-Xiao Zhang, David Tanner, Rachel Houtz, PAUL FULDA, James Hamlin, Yoonseok Lee, Jeff Dror, YUXUAN WANG

description: Research for Doctoral Dissertation
instructors: Jeff Dror, Guido Mueller, Heather Ray, Wei Xue, Richard Hennig, Khandker Muttalib, Imre Bartos, PAUL FULDA, Jeffrey Andrews, Rasul Gazizulin, Chunjing Jia, Xiao-Xiao Zhang, Dmitrii Maslov, Philip Chang, BingKan Xue, Jacobo Konigsberg, Laura Blecha, Sumita Bhaduri-McIntosh, Peter Hirschfeld, DOMINIQUE LAROCHE, James Hamlin, Richard Woodard, Pierre Sikivie, Christopher Stanton, David Tanner, Tarek Saab, Rachel Houtz, Yoonseok Lee, Amlan Biswas, Desika Narayanan

description: Atomic binding, crystalline structure, diffraction and reciprocal lattice, lattice vibration, phonons, electrons in solids, energy bands, semiconductors.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHY 4604; Coreq: PHY 4523.

description: The physics of biological systems, including physics of proteins and nucleic acids, biomolecular motors and diffusional signaling and sensing. Important experimental tools such as magnetic resonance and synchrotron x-ray crystallography are also discussed. (WR)
prerequisites: Credits: 3; Prereq: one year of introductory physics (PHY 2053/2054, PHY 2048/2049, or the equivalent) and one year of calculus (MAC 2311/2312, or the equivalent).

description: Physics of collective phenomena in condensed matter systems: electron-electron and electron-phonon interactions, magnetism, superconductivity, and quantum transport.
prerequisites: Prereq: PHZ 6426.
